What do you think this site is about wit: A collection of dessert recipes
What stands out to you visually?: The strong text hierarchy and sections divided by lines, which looks very clean and clear. I also notice that the recipes are all easy to find along the left side. While I appreciate the visual language of the website, I also notice that there are no photographs with the recipes on the main page, which stands out because it's something I would expect to see.
What is unclear or confusing?: On the top menu, I don't quite understand what is meant by "How?" and "Recent", and I don't know what to expect in those sections. When I go to the "How" page, I don't know if it's something that needs its own page–maybe it can be combined with the "About" page.
Do you ever feel lost? If so, where? Wha: Since the recipes change upon hovering, I did not realize for a while that you could also click on the recipe names and go to an additional page with all the steps. Maybe there is a way to make that clearer?
Is there sufficient content to understan: If the project is just to document your recipes as you make them, kind of like a blog, then the site feels like it has enough to start with. If it is meant to be an archive with many dessert recipes, I think it needs a few more, and once you add more, it would be nice to have sections for different types of desserts–cookies, cakes, etc.
Where are the moments where you want mor: Tools and time are not currently listed, but I assume those will be filled out soon. Otherwise, the recipes feel clear and easy to understand with the current amount of information.
Is the site well-structured and visually: Yes, all the recipes are together, they change on hover, and the format is clean and sharp. The title and menu are in a position that is clear and makes sense.
Are there any areas where images, assets: While I like the drawn image in the "About" section, there are no others like that across the site. Maybe you could incorporate a few more of those (one option is to have them with the previews for each recipe, or you can include some as decorative elements).
Name any specific formal aspects of the: Typographic hierarchy and layout are working well. The design is minimal yet strong.

Some of the type could be a bit smaller, especially for body text. I also think it would be nice to have images of each recipe show up when you hover over the recipe previews, whether those images are photos or drawings.
Do links, buttons, and interactions beha: For the most part, yes. I noticed some pages didn't have the top menu working, but those pages are also still incomplete, so I assume that's why. The "Recent" button also doesn't go anywhere as of now.
Are there any broken, unclear, or frustr: The "Recent" button, as mentioned above. I also almost didn't notice that you could click on "Are you ready?" on the "About" page, so maybe that could be underlined or styled in some way to make it clearer.
Does anything feel awkward, or unintuiti: On the "About" page, while I like the "Are you ready?", it doesn't make sense since you already see all the recipes on the home page. It could work if it came before the home page, but that also doesn't feel necessary. Maybe you could word that text differently so it makes sense on the About page (something like "Go to recipes" or even "Let's go!" could work).
How does the website load? Are there spe: Loads well for me.
Describe the task you undertook:: Making the panna cotta
Were you able to complete the task?: Mostly (note: I didn't have gelatin, but I knew that going into the recipe and didn't consider it in this answer)
What helped and/or prevented you from co: I didn't realize the recipe required berries when I started it because it wasn't listed in the preview/ingredients list. I wasn't sure if it was better to use sugar cubes (rather than regular sugar) since they were in the photo, but the ingredient list simply said "sugar". I also found it a bit inconvenient to go back and forth from the preview page and the recipe steps page in order to remember how much of each ingredient was needed–it would be great if you repeated the amounts either within the recipe steps, or just copied the ingredients onto the recipe page as well. In general, however, it was helpful to see the ingredients and time it would take before making the recipe, so I could ensure I had everything needed and enough time before I started.
Identify 2–3 things that are working wel:
1. The fact that some ingredients are optional or can be substituted aligns with the fact that the recipes are supposed to be simple.
 2. The previews and recipes are both clear and to the point, with no unnecessary extra information. 
 3. I like the tone of voice on the website. It feels very enthusiastic and encouraging for the user. Combined with the clarity of the site, it feels quite easy to get started on a recipe.
Identify 2–3 areas for improvement:Focus:
1. The "How?" page could be combined with the "About" page, since it is really just more information about the website.
 2. It should be clearer that you can click onto the recipe name to see the full recipe. For a while as I went through the site, I didn't realize this was an option.
 3. I would like to see images in the previews, not necessarily all of them immediately (since I do like the simple clean design), but perhaps when you hover over each recipe name.

What do you think this site is about wit: It appears to me that the site is about Japan because the kanji on the homepage is centered and very apparent at first glance. Scrolling down, various types of flowers are featured in grid blocks, so I assume it's mainly about Japanese plants/flora/soils (by regions).
What stands out to you visually?: First, the animated/moving background caught my attention and pulled me in right away. I feel as if I am transported to that place directly, so, in a sense, it is engaging. Secondly, the interface of the homepage is immersive because the image is fixed and doesn't budge as you scroll down, while everything else flows normally.
What is unclear or confusing?: If there could be more correlation between the main title background and the topic, it could reinforce your ideas more and make the page feel more cohesive, as I feel like as of right now, it does give a strong impression of Japan as a destination, but since your topic is about flowers and regional flora, clips that showcase each region’s seasonal plants could be far more effective.
Do you ever feel lost? If so, where? Wha: When I just entered the site, I was a bit uncertain about what to do next. There are no visual cues or scroll indicators on the first page, which makes me ask myself whether there are any elements I need to interact with before proceeding to the next (I initially tried to click the kanji title and the tagline). I thought maybe using an icon like an arrow at the bottom of the first page could make the direction clearer to help guide the user through the site, probably also because the first page doesn't have a header or a menu, so maybe adding a few small icons could help.
Is there sufficient content to understan: It has a decent amount of information across all pages, a good general introduction to the regional flowers when clicking on "learn more about (region)" or when interacting with the arrow next to the flower images, which provides the user with a short overview of the soil specialty of each region.
Where are the moments where you want mor: I would definitely love to learn more about each flower that is featured on the homepage, because they aren't all mentioned in the "learn more" section, their unique shapes, colors and cultural significance are so intriguing! I think there’s huge potential to make them stand out more; one idea that would make the site more engaging and interactive might be to turn each flower into a flip card (or hover/tap card) component.
Is the site well-structured and visually: Yes, the overall layout is clean with full width background that makes the homepage look quite cinematic, and a larger image also tends to pull the user in a lot. The repeatable components of the flower blocks in the grid make the page look organized and less over the place.
Are there any areas where images, assets: The structure works fine the way it is; the only thing that bothers me a bit is that some images might seem inconsistent in terms of quality. Some of them are very neat, while others could be a bit lower, and having these two types of images side by side makes the quality very noticeable.
Name any specific formal aspects of the: The typeface complements the topic and the overall page aesthetics. It looks elegant and refined, which highlights the beauty of the flowers. The flower image on the homepage works well, too, as the grey background helps isolate the blooms from distractions and draws attention to their structure, shape, and color.

Currently, I feel like the layout and structure are solid, but the page can be slow to load. Images take a while to appear, and the animated background often makes the page stall for a few seconds. Making them load faster would enhance the overall experience and underwhelm the user's experience, as it would cause less frustration.
Do links, buttons, and interactions beha: Yes, works fine. All the interactive buttons and links to other pages are clearly labeled and take me to the exact destination I expect.
Are there any broken, unclear, or frustr: So far, all good.
Does anything feel awkward, or unintuiti: Nothing of the sort, the navigation is intuitive, and labels are descriptive enough to avoid confusion.
How does the website load? Are there spe: Overall, the website is a bit slow, especially the animated images, which take roughly twice as long to load as static images and can sometimes impact other users’ experiences. This slowdown can cause pages to feel unresponsive and may interfere with scrolling or interacting with page elements.
Describe the task you undertook:: I was curious about Hoshu’s flowers, so I clicked “Learn more about Hoshu.” It took me to a page with several image entries showing local flora and the region’s rich soil.
What helped and/or prevented you from co: The "Learn more about Hoshu" highlighted with a small arrow under the description part on the home page for each region was helpful, as it directed me to a new page filled with additional information about that exact place (with the dominant/abundant flowers, soils, etc).
Identify 2–3 things that are working wel:
- My favorite part of the site is still the overlay scrolling image, where the pictures remain fixed while the containing box and its text move as you scroll. It makes the page feel immersive and draws attention to the content without distracting from the visuals.
 - The overall layout works well: there isn’t an overload of information, the text complements the background image, and the repeating image grid is spaced comfortably rather than feeling cramped, which helps maintain visual balance and readability.
Identify 2–3 areas for improvement:Focus:
- The site is slightly slow; to avoid disrupting the user experience, consider replacing animated images with stills. If animation is important, make them passive still images that activate into motion only on user interaction (hover or click). This reduces constant CPU load and helps prevent crashes or lag.
 -This is just a suggestion, but increase contrast to improve visual appeal and readability. Introduce a complementary accent color to the light beige and black/grey palette; you could use it selectively (for example, on kanji headings) so those words stand out from descriptions. If you prefer to keep the current palette, boost typographic contrast instead: increase font weight or size for kanji, or use a darker/lighter tone to create clearer separation between headings and body text.

What do you think this site is about wit: Different sounds/sonic experiences found in varying parts of Markham
What stands out to you visually?: The style of cursor is customized, the colours and minimalism of the map, the pulsating red dots showing different sonic locations on the map and connecting lines between them
What is unclear or confusing?: I find the website very clear and I'm not confused at all. The pop up when you first enter the site immediately explains the purpose of the site, the red dots on the mind guide you to what to do, and the information/metadata once you've clicked a sound is very clear and helpful. All the steps and instructions are very clear.
Do you ever feel lost? If so, where? Wha: I don't feel lost. It immediately made sense to me how to navigate and engage with the website. The instructions, labels and visual cues (red dots) are very helpful.
Is there sufficient content to understan: I think 10 sonic entries is enough for the basis of an archival website, especially because there is the option for users to submit their own sounds in different locations. I think once the sound entries are more spread out on the map, it might feel more "complete", but it still works well the way it is now too.
Where are the moments where you want mor: The metadata included is very helpful and is a great addition along with listening to the sounds. I think without the metadata it might feel incomplete but I think it gives me all the information I need to enjoy it and explore the sounds. I don't know what more information I would want or need. I thought that maybe adding the sources of the sounds (ex. car traffics, people talking) could be insightful but I now think overexplaining it would detract from the listening experience. So I think there is a perfect amount of information and there's nothing else that's missing in my opinion.
Is the site well-structured and visually: I think so. The structure is clear and visually neat. The aesthetic and colours are cohesive throughout the whole site. I do think that with how strong your home page is, the "about" and "submit" pages might benefit from having more visual interest so they don't seem so empty in comparison (maybe through adding some of the colours on your map?)
Are there any areas where images, assets: The style (colours, typography etc.) and site components (ex. navbar buttons, mouse cursor) are consistent throughout all the pages.
Name any specific formal aspects of the: I think the analogue feel of the site is working well, with the sans and slab serif old computer style typography, minimalist colour scheme and chunky cursor reminiscent of an old GPS. It works well with the aesthetic of old websites and connects back to map/navigational imagery. I think if it looked too modern it wouldn't work as well. I also think the minimalism of the site also works well because it isn't so visually distracting that the sonic nature of the site gets lost or seems secondary. I also think the minimalism of the map works well for the same reason; it's not too detailed and so the sonic experience becomes more important and prominent.

I think the site name "Markham Sound Map" could be a larger point size because some of the subheadings of pages such as "About this project" sit in a similar place in the visual hierarchy so it might benefit from pushing it a bit more. I think the site might benefit from more padding around the navigational buttons as they seem quite close to the header bar of the site. Additionally, because the submit page is so empty, maybe making the buttons and the answer fields a bit bigger could help balance the page out and provide more space to click on with the cursor. Or alternatively, you could just moved the content down so it takes up more space and everything isn't only on the top of the "about" and "submit" pages.
Do links, buttons, and interactions beha: Yes
Are there any broken, unclear, or frustr: No
Does anything feel awkward, or unintuiti: No
How does the website load? Are there spe: The only thing that sometimes loads slowly when zooming in or out is the map. Other than that, everything else seems to load just fine, such as the initial popup when entering the site.
Describe the task you undertook:: I found a specific sonic entry
What helped and/or prevented you from co: The clear instructions, flashing red dots as visual cues on the maps all helped to complete the task
Identify 2–3 things that are working wel:
The red flashing dots work well to guide the user to the next step and are very engaging, clear and effective 
 The metadata is very insightful and helps inform the sonic experience, it is engaging, clear and effective 
 The submit function is a great idea and very engaging as a user and also helps expand the sonic archive and the potential global reach of this website
Identify 2–3 areas for improvement:Focus:
Because your home page is so great and visually engaging, the two other site pages feel emptier in comparison. Maybe bringing over some elements from your home page like the map colours/forms/patterns could help unify them a bit more?
 Making the submission boxes a bit larger could help make them easier to use as there is more clickable area for the mouse and it could be easier to read in a slightly larger point size.

What do you think this site is about wit: Although the headline is somewhat vague, based on the items shown on the homepage, I’d assume it’s about essential objects people always bring with them whenever they travel or move far away from home.
What stands out to you visually?: The color palette used for the items— intense, highly saturated green and blue, with a subtle halftone texture- gives each item a vivid and bold presence. The details are clearer and definitely more eye-catching, as the contours are accentuated and so are the small details.
What is unclear or confusing?: The title feels awkwardly phrased. I initially had trouble understanding what "Must Items from Home" means— the wording doesn’t sound natural.
Do you ever feel lost? If so, where? Wha: I think the main issue right now is that the items feel a bit all over the place. On the homepage, they’re presented as a long list of links with almost no context or direct connection between them—except for the fact that they’re all labeled as “must-bring” items from home. Because no clear grouping or personal story tying them together, the page ends up feeling scattered despite being organized in a grid block. Perhaps adding a brief subhead beneath the headline to provide context would help, even though the page "about" already exists.
Is there sufficient content to understan: Only three images/blocks are missing, which doesn’t really affect my experience. The rest of the site feels complete: I enjoy it since each image is linked to a new page with the full interview of an international student, offering concise context for why each item was chosen. There isn’t a traditional header, but the cross icon serves as a suitable alternative, offering enough context about the site’s purpose.
Where are the moments where you want mor: This is just my opinion, but if we focused more on the items than the people. For example, by adding longer descriptions or fun facts about each object, its significance would feel stronger. Right now, I come away knowing more about the person than the things they bring (not that it's a bad thing).
Is the site well-structured and visually: I would say it is strong, because the repeatable component is clearly defined: a 3x3 grid of image blocks spanning the page. Each block contains its own repeated internal structure, giving the site a consistent, cohesive sense of shape and form.
Are there any areas where images, assets: No, everything is well-structured and images are used effectively to maintain a consistency across the site.
Name any specific formal aspects of the: The typography works very well with the imagery. Against the halftone-textured images, the monospaced descriptions reinforce a retro, print-inspired aesthetic. Especially the rigid rhythm of the monospaced type contrasts nicely with the organic shapes of the objects.

I’d give the “Save this Item” box a larger margin, since it’s currently too close to the edge and the text feels cramped. I’d also move it directly below the image so it’s more eye-catching and easier to interact with.
Do links, buttons, and interactions beha: There were no problems. I was directed to the pages I expected, and each image took me straight to the students’ interviews. The menu checklist worked the same way and was a useful feature, as it gathered all the images for which I checked the box.
Are there any broken, unclear, or frustr: So far no.
Does anything feel awkward, or unintuiti: So far no.
How does the website load? Are there spe: The website is fast, there were no crashes or bugs. All elements, assets, and images loaded quickly and ran perfectly.
Describe the task you undertook:: I tested the save-item feature by checking several items. I saved the stuffed animal and the teapot to my collection, then opened the checklist from the menu. Both items appeared on the checklist. I later unsaved the kettle, and it was removed from the list.
What helped and/or prevented you from co: It updates immediately when clicked, and the checklist reflects changes without delay.
Identify 2–3 things that are working wel:
-I found the save-item box very engaging — it felt like online shopping, adding items to a cart, and gave me a sense of excitement as I moved from one item to the next. It’s also helpful that items can be removed from the checklist.
 
 -The images are well edited and have a retro, nostalgic vibe. I also like the hover interaction: when you move the mouse over an image, it becomes more lively and grows slightly larger, which makes the site feel more engaging.
Identify 2–3 areas for improvement:Focus:
-I would suggest fixing the page's margin structure and positioning the "Save this Item" box directly below the image so that the box becomes more apparent and visible to the user's eye, rather than being placed at the bottom with minimal margin.
 
 -Instead of laying out full-block grids on the homepage, consider dividing them into clear sections or secondary sub-genres. For example, grouping related images—and converting those groups into carousels. This would free up space on the homepage and give you more room to experiment with the layout.

What do you think this site is about wit: When I first browsed the site, I didn’t read the text but focused solely on the visual elements, and I was curious about what the five rectangles with different aspect ratios meant. Then, the moment later I realized the site is related to the theme of film, and I concluded that these rectangles represented movie’s screen ratios.
What stands out to you visually?: it looks like a professional analysis website that contains all the necessary information With its highly intuitive layout and simple yet comprehensive text.
What is unclear or confusing?: As mentioned earlier, When users browse the website in visual aspect, without reading texts, users might be curious what the rectangles represent. However, this has the advantage of prompting users to read the detailed descriptions and encouraging them to analyze the site more thoroughly.
Do you ever feel lost? If so, where? Wha:
Some images are listed on the screen but are not visible due to an error. (In seconde image of CLERKS, LEAVING LAS VEGAS, THE BLAIR WITCH PROJECT, 
 SQUID AND THE WHALE, MOTHER!, MID90S, etc.) Also, I would like the text describing each aspect ratio—which appears when you click the rectangle—to remain fixed in view even when scrolling down.
Is there sufficient content to understan: I believe that the information currently available on the website is sufficient to provide a good understanding of the subject. Adding further details would offer a more in-depth insight and give users the opportunity to engage with the films even if they have not seen them.
Where are the moments where you want mor:
When I read the text on the website’s introduction page, it states, “These platforms are not just specifications of cameras or the size of a theater screen. Each format generates a different kind of image through its own texture, resolution, and frame.” However, so far, the site only contains information on the aspect ratios used in each film, and there seems to be a lack of information on how these aspect ratios influenced the films’ visuals.
 If, upon clicking on each film and the website provided an explanation of how the aspect ratio visually enhances the film and highlights its key features, or information on why the director chose to use that ratio and the circumstances behind that decision, it would become a far more comprehensive resource.
Is the site well-structured and visually: Overall, the site has been designed to be simple and straightforward. In particular, the typographic hierarchy is well implemented (for example, the font size of the film titles is the largest, whilst the remaining details are arranged in smaller sizes below them), making it possible to see at a glance what each section of text signifies. Furthermore, when clicking on a film in the list, the fact that the titles of the other films—which have not been clicked—are displayed with reduced opacity demonstrates a keen attention to detail.
Are there any areas where images, assets: All elements—particularly the photographs, which are closely related to the theme of this website and require careful attention—have been included in each category in appropriate proportions.
Name any specific formal aspects of the: The function that the five different sizes of the rectangles correspond to the screen ratios used in each film seems to help users understand the theme of this website more easily. When users encounter aspect ratios such as 16mm, 35mm, vistavation, and 70mm in text form, it’s quite difficult to gauge their actual size even if they already have knowledge about this. However, by visualizing these aspect ratios so simply and clearly using rectangles, the overall understanding of the subject is enhanced. Additionally, while the design- with a blck background and text rendered entirely in white – could easily appear monotonous, the colors in the movie screenshots seem to add a touch of sophistication to the design. (Although some of the featured films are black-and-white.)

The website is basically organized into three sections (far left: categories; the aspect ratio system on the left, divided in a 1:2 ratio; and explanations of the ratios along with a movie list on the right). There is an inconvenience in that you have to scroll down to view a specific movie, and then scroll back up to click on another movie. Furthermore, if additional content is added to address the lack of information mentioned earlier, the scroll length will increase, and this structure could cause user fatigue. Therefore, it would be beneficial to reconsider the layout of the list so that users can click on another movie entry immediately while viewing the description of the movie they clicked on.

What do you think this site is about wit: At first, I thought the site was a virtual museum or gallery guide because of the arrows and navigation cues. It felt like it was guiding me through a space. It took me a bit longer to understand that it was actually an archive, and also working as a set of instructions for people who might be new to gallery spaces.
What stands out to you visually?: The header and interview layout stand out because they feel clear and structured. The grid and the separations between the interview boxes create a strong sense of visual hierarchy and organization. Making the content easier to scan and helps guide the eye across the page.
What is unclear or confusing?: At first, when clicking on the arrows, the user is taken to another page with four arrows that suggest movement in different directions. This gave me the impression of a 3D space or map, like I was navigating through a virtual environment. That was confusing at first because it was not immediately clear what each direction represented or how the navigation worked, so it took some time to understand how to move through the site.
Do you ever feel lost? If so, where? Wha: Yes, I felt lost when interacting with the large arrows on the landing page and then again in what seems to be a menu or gallery space with four arrows. That section gave the impression of movement, but it wasn’t immediately clear where each direction would take me or how the system was organized. I think using more conventional elements, like clearly labeled tiles or previews for each gallery, would help make the navigation more intuitive and reduce confusion for first-time users.
Is there sufficient content to understan: In terms of the interviews, I believe having two out of four is enough to showcase the current progress and to understand the direction of the project. However, on the About page, the site is described as both an archive and a practical guide, which was not entirely clear to me. Instead, it felt more like an editorial piece made up of artist interviews. Clarifying the purpose or aligning the description more closely with the actual content would help the project feel more complete and consistent.
Where are the moments where you want mor: The moments where I wanted more information were related to guidance for beginners. As I mentioned before, I was expecting a more straightforward guide that outlines clear steps or practical advice. While it is interesting to see the perspectives and approaches of different professionals, it can feel confusing and overwhelming for someone new to gallery spaces to go through all that information without a simple, structured guide. Adding a clear section with basic steps, tips, or a short summary for beginners would make the project more accessible and easier to understand.
Is the site well-structured and visually: Overall, the site is visually strong and partially well-structured. The top navigation and interview pages are clear, organized, and easy to follow. However, the landing page and the gallery space with the four arrows create confusion, which affects the overall structure and usability.
Are there any areas where images, assets: Yes, aside from the landing page and the four-arrow gallery, there are other inconsistencies. The interview pages feel clear and structured, while the earlier sections rely on more abstract navigation, creating a disconnect in how the site is experienced. These issues are mostly related to structure and navigation rather than visual assets. There is also a mismatch between the About page description and the actual content, which feels more editorial than a guide or archive.
Name any specific formal aspects of the: In terms of typography, layout, and images, I think the selection is well curated. Choosing a sans-serif typeface helps with clarity and readability, especially for longer text like the interviews. The structure of the website, with a clear top navigation bar, hero section are well defined and creates a strong overall framework. The layout of the interview pages is also effective, as it organizes the content in a way that is easy to follow. The images support the content without overwhelming it, helping maintain a good balance between text and visuals.

Overall, most formal aspects of the site are working well. However, the landing page and the gallery space with the arrows could be refined further. These sections introduce a type of navigation that feels less clear compared to the rest of the site. Improving these areas to better align with the more structured layout of the interview pages would help enhance the overall user experience and make the site easier to understand from the beginning. I also did not notice a footer, which could help provide closure to the page and reinforce the site’s structure.
Do links, buttons, and interactions beha: Yes, links, buttons, and interactions generally behave as expected. Once the user understands the navigation system, the interactions work consistently and allow the user to move through the site without major issues.
Are there any broken, unclear, or frustr: No, there are no broken or frustrating interactions. Overall, the interactions work as expected once the user understands how the navigation system functions.
Does anything feel awkward, or unintuiti: I might sound a bit repetitive, but the issues mentioned before, especially the landing page arrows and the four-arrow gallery, feel awkward and unintuitive. These elements are not immediately clear and can make navigation confusing at first. There is also a mismatch between the About page, which presents the site as a guide, and the actual content, which feels more like editorial interview-based material.
How does the website load? Are there spe: In terms of loading, everything works well. The pages load smoothly, and images appear without noticeable delays.
Describe the task you undertook:: I chose both the archive and instructional testing. As a first-time gallery user, I was not able to find a specific entry or item, nor was I able to follow a clear step or process. While the site was informative in terms of getting to know established artists and gallerists, it did not offer the type of structured navigation or guidance needed to complete either of these tasks.
What helped and/or prevented you from co: What prevented me from completing the task was the lack of a clear guide. The site mainly presents interviews where people share their experiences, but it does not provide direct advice or structured steps for first-time users. This made it difficult to follow a process or find specific information.
Identify 2–3 things that are working wel:
1.- The structure of the interview pages is clear and easy to follow.
 2.- Overall layout is well organized, especially once inside the main content sections.
 3.- Top navigation helps guide the user through the site.
 4.- Typography is clear and readable, supporting longer text effectively.
 5.- The images complement the content without overwhelming it.
 T6.- The interview format is engaging and informative, especially in presenting the perspectives of artists and gallerists.
Identify 2–3 areas for improvement:Focus:
1.- The arrow-based navigation on the landing page could be clearer, as it suggests movement but does not clearly indicate where each direction leads. Consider adding labels, previews, or brief descriptions to help users understand what each option represents.
 
 2. The gallery space with the four arrows could be more intuitive, as it feels like a navigation hub but lacks a clear structure. Consider replacing it with a grid or tile layout that visually presents each section and makes choices more explicit.
 
 3. The purpose of the site described in the About page could be more aligned with the actual content. Consider clarifying whether the site functions as a guide/archive or reframing it as an editorial/interview-based project to better match the user experience.

What do you think this site is about wit: Even without reading the text carefully, I could tell that this was a food-related website because the names of the dessert were highlighted in large, bold text.

What is unclear or confusing?: The website’s title, the food names in the left-side frame, and the ‘Ingredients’, ‘Serving’, ‘Tools’ and ‘Time’ sections in the right-side frame all feature bold text, making it unclear which elements are the main focus and resulting in an unclear typographic hierarchy.
Do you ever feel lost? If so, where? Wha: The text weight is set too high for too many elements, which makes it hard to know where to click when viewing the website’s homepage as a whole. To improve this, applying bold formatting to just one element—such as the website title or the dessert names in the left-hand frame—would enhance readability.
Is there sufficient content to understan: All the components of the main page—such as the ingredients required to make each dessert (which are displayed when the mouse is hovered over them), the number of servings, the necessary utensils and the preparation time—are visible on a single screen at once aligns perfectly with the essential elements of a recipe, making it highly suitable for the website’s theme.
Where are the moments where you want mor: As this site archives recipes created in a unique way rather than presenting standardised dessert recipes, providing information that highlights the differences from existing recipes allows users to compare the two and gain a deeper understanding of the recipes through this process.
Is the site well-structured and visually: overall, layout of the website is neat and well-organised, and its purpose is clear, given that it is a text-based website that prioritises the user’s reading experience; paying attention to typographical details such as font size and line spacing would help to improve the site’s readability.
Are there any areas where images, assets: The sizes of the images attached to each recipe are inconsistent, making it difficult to create a sense of cohesion. Furthermore, at the bottom of the pages that appear when clicking the ‘About’ and ‘How?’ buttons, there is an ‘Are You Ready?’ button that returns the user to the main page. If these buttons share the same functionality, they should maintain a consistent design so that users can expect the same response when they click them.
Name any specific formal aspects of the: On the main screen, the dessert list is displayed on the left, while the essential information required for the recipe is shown on the right; this split layout design avoids confusion and gives a sense of organisation.

The text in the right-side frame, which is divided into columns, appears unbalanced because the bottom margin is wider than the top margin. The most significant issue is that the information beneath ‘Ingredients’, ‘Serving’, ‘Tools’ and ‘Time’ appears scattered due to the wide margins, making it look as though the details are unrelated. I would suggest reducing the bottom margin value to eliminate the vast empty space beneath the text would help prevent an awkward flow of text.
Do links, buttons, and interactions beha: All links, buttons behave as expectation. I was particularly impressed by the feature that takes you back to the home screen when you press the button that appears at the end of a sentence on the ‘How’ page.
Are there any broken, unclear, or frustr: When I click on the 6 “Lemon Ricotta Cake”, I should be taken to the recipe page, but a 404 error appears.
Does anything feel awkward, or unintuiti: It seems we need to fix the awkward issue where the same text appears when clicking the Home button as when hovering over the Tiramisu button.
How does the website load? Are there spe: The images on all pages except 04 Torta Caprese, 05 Biscotti, 06 Lemon Ricotta Cake and ‘Stick to the basics’ load at a steady speed.
Describe the task you undertook:: I was looking for a recipe for tiramisu that offers a different approach to the usual one. I’d like a step-by-step recipe that explains each stage of the process.
Were you able to complete the task?: The detailed instructions, which guide through each step of the process, helped me achieve my goal. Furthermore, the images illustrating the cooking steps at each stage provide users with a visual experience.
What helped and/or prevented you from co: The text which is appeard in the recipt page to provide a detailed account of the cooking process, and the accompanying images offer users an engaging way to explore recipes. One slight drawback is that it is not always clear which parts of the process differ from a standard recipe. To address this, using a different colour or increasing the text weight to highlight sections describing unique ingredients or methods would help draw attention to them.
Identify 2–3 things that are working wel:
1. I believe that including details such as the necessary tools, precise quantities of ingredients and the time required—in line with the website’s purpose of providing recipes ㄱ the expectations of users browsing the site.
 2. A key element of a recipe is ensuring that users can easily understand the content, both visually and in terms of the text itself. I believe the use of a sans-serif font to create a simple feel, combined with a straightforward layout, has been successfully applied to this website.
Identify 2–3 areas for improvement:Focus:
1. Typographic hierarchy
 As a website consists largely of text, it is essential to ensure good readability; therefore, attention must be paid to font size, alignment and rags.
 2. Interactive dessert-making steps 
 Currently, there are two images within each step on the screen; reducing this to a single image and adding a button—such as an arrow—that displays the next image when clicked will create a system where the user and the website interact with each other, giving a more organised feel.

What do you think this site is about wit: As soon as I visited the website, I noticed a description on the homepage explaining what the site archives, which immediately made it clear that it was a site related to films.
What stands out to you visually?: By eliminating all non-essential elements related to this topic and retaining only the essentials, the simple layout design ensures that the user can view the information they require without any visual distractions.
What is unclear or confusing?: It feels a bit odd that the film’s details page is generated on a new page.
Do you ever feel lost? If so, where? Wha: Each time a film is clicked, rather than simply displaying the next page on the existing page, a new tab opens to show the film’s details page. Although this doesn’t disrupt the flow of the page, it creates the inconvenience of having to close numerous tabs later on. Furthermore, the most noticeable issue is that there is currently no way to return to the previous page after viewing the film’s details page. To return to the previous page, users must click on the relevant category again to navigate back to the page where the film was listed. As this method may cause user fatigue, it would be beneficial to add a feature such as an arrow linking back to the previous page.
Is there sufficient content to understan: Clicking on each film displays several screenshots; these images do not merely show random scenes from the film, but rather highlight aspects relevant to the category (for example, if you click on a film within a category showcasing unique costumes, you will see images of the characters’ outfits from that film), which helps users understand the content better. Furthermore, the main page features an image with links to the four categories, creating a sense that all pages within the website are interconnected.
Where are the moments where you want mor: The film’s details page includes information on the country of production, the studio, the director, and the team members within that category. Considering the website’s aims of (a) showcasing the work of production designers, their teams, and other artists working in film; and (b) provide inspiration to other filmmakers and artists; it would be more professional and would enhance the depth of the website if the page included behind-the-scenes insights into the film’s production process or research into how specific elements were created.
Is the site well-structured and visually: Essentially, it contains all the key content and has a well-organised feel. At present, there is some margin at the bottom of the website layout; however, if there were three or more films on a category page and the screen were filled with images from those films, it might create a different impression to the current design.
Are there any areas where images, assets: All images have consistent aspect ratios, and the layout is uniform throughout, with no inconsistencies.
Name any specific formal aspects of the: As this is a website dedicated to archiving unique film sets, character costumes, make-up and props, images play a crucial role. I believe the layout of the film detail pages—which is image-centric, with text containing information placed below—aligns well with the website’s objectives. Furthermore, the fact that the image a user clicks on is displayed in a larger size, whilst the rest are shown in a smaller size, enhances readability.

Name any specific formal aspects of the:
The bold red sans-serif typesetting and black type on the white background work really well. The typographic hierarchy is clear, and it effectively distinguishes each part by varying the color, weight, and size of the type.
 
 What I found particularly effective was that the website maintains consistency even across varied layouts in each section. 1.Alignment of subheaders and descriptions & alignment of small headers.

How does the website load? Are there spe: 1. In the All Cards section, it takes a while to load all of the cards because there are a lot of images, but the wait was not that inconvenient. (2) Additionally, in the All Cards section, when clicking on a specific tarot card, the two larger images at the top load a little slowly.
Describe the task you undertook:: This works as both an archive site and a tool. I was able to view the full archive of the tarot cards and use it as a resource to learn each card’s role and interpretation.
Were you able to complete the task?: As an archive, it was easy to complete the task. The tarot descriptions were detailed and well-structured in the archive section, which made it easy to find specific items.
What helped and/or prevented you from co: As I mentioned, since the intro and archive sections contain text-heavy paragraphs as a learning tool for how to read tarot, I would suggest clarifying the alignment between section headers and their descriptions to help users read more easily without confusion.
Identify 2–3 things that are working wel:
1. There is clear and sufficient information on how to read tarot, and the typographic hierarchy in the text-heavy sections is well handled (clear distinction between titles, sub headers, and body text).
 
 2. The overall layout is varied yet consistent. As I mentioned, even though there are a few different layouts across sections, it keeps the experience visually engaging and prevents the website from feeling boring.
 
 3.The quiz & “card of the day” section is especially engaging and effective. It encourages users to revisit the site, since they can get different results and test their knowledge every day.
Identify 2–3 areas for improvement:Focus:
1.Alignment: I would improve the alignment between the heading and the description so that there is a clearer distinction from the following content.
 
 2.Typesetting:For text-heavy paragraphs, I would recommend using left alignment, as center alignment can be a bit difficult and distracting to read in longer blocks of text.
 
 3.Navigation (Go Back & Linked Buttons):
 On the About and Intro pages, I would suggest including a “Go Back” button for easier navigation.
 I’d also recommend adding the About page as a separate item in the menu, and possibly including a linked button that leads to the Intro section.
